elements.sent-2411-001: Large Language Models (LLMs) have become integral to various software engineering tasks, including code generation, bug detection, and repair.
elements.sent-2411-002: To evaluate model performance in these domains, numerous bug benchmarks containing real-world bugs from software projects have been developed.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, a growing concern within the software engineering community is that these benchmarks may not reliably reflect true LLM performance due to the risk of data leakage.
elements.sent-2411-004: Despite this concern, limited research has been conducted to quantify the impact of potential leakage.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we systematically evaluate popular LLMs to assess their susceptibility to data leakage from widely used bug benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-006: To identify potential leakage, we use multiple metrics, including a study of benchmark membership within commonly used training datasets, as well as analyses of negative log-likelihood and n-gram accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our findings show that certain models, in particular \cgen, exhibit significant evidence of memorization in widely used benchmarks like \defj, while newer models trained on larger datasets like \llama exhibit limited signs of leakage.
elements.sent-2411-008: These results highlight the need for careful benchmark selection and the adoption of robust metrics to adequately assess models capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-009: Large language models (LLMs) have become ubiquitous for various software engineering tasks.
elements.sent-2411-010: Assessing these models' abilities in context, beyond the basic evaluations typically performed upon release (e.g., on HumanEval~\cite{chen2021humaneval ), benefits from realistic benchmarks that represent real-world software development tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Two significant such tasks are bug finding, through automated fault localization (FL)~\cite{flSurvey ; and bug fixing, through automated program repair~\cite{aprCACM (APR).}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The Software Engineering community has released numerous bug benchmarks for evaluating success on these tasks, consisting of real bugs from open-source software projects.
elements.sent-2411-013: Notable such datasets include, for example, \defj~\cite{defects4j (\java) and \bugspy~\cite{bugsinpy (\py); similarly, ML researchers recently introduced SWEBench~\cite{swebench .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Data leakage refers to the use of information during model training that would not normally be available during prediction, leading to inflated performance metrics that misrepresent a model's true effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-015: The programs and bugs in many benchmarks and solutions have been publicly accessible for years, increasing the chance they were incorporated into LLM training data.
elements.sent-2411-016: For instance, the widely-used \defj dataset, based on popular \java projects, was first released in 2014.
elements.sent-2411-017: To illustrate, consider the example shown in \Cref{fig:motivation .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Here, we prompted \cgen (6 billion parameters) to predict the next tokens based on an incomplete snippet (highlighted in yellow) from the bug-fix file for \defj bug \#39 in the Lang project.
elements.sent-2411-019: The generated text, highlighted in blue, shows that \cgen reproduced the remainder of the solution file verbatim, including specific comments (lines 31 and 35), token by token.
elements.sent-2411-020: This exact reproduction strongly suggests that the model has memorized the benchmark solution.
elements.sent-2411-021: Such behavior underscores the need for caution when using benchmarks like \defj, as they may lead to misleading conclusions about a model’s generalizability.
elements.sent-2411-022: Researchers in other domains, like NLP or ML, have attempted to quantify data leakage in their datasets~\cite{xu2024benchmarkingbenchmarkleakagelarge,li2023estimatingcontaminationperplexityquantifying .}
elements.sent-2411-023: To the best of our knowledge, this effort has not been undertaken for popular bug datasets in Software Engineering.
elements.sent-2411-025: LLMs are often pre-trained by organizations that do not disclose their datasets, the total volume of data, nor model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-026: Consequently, determining whether a benchmark has been directly included or merely mirrored in the model’s training set is difficult.
elements.sent-2411-027: Even verifying the presence of data only establishes that the model has seen it, not necessarily that it has memorized it, particularly when it is one training sample among billions.
elements.sent-2411-028: Thus, we ask: Are large language models memorizing bug benchmarks? We systematically evaluate popular LLMs to quantify their susceptibility to data leakage on widely used bug benchmarks and raise awareness of the risks associated with using established benchmarks, which may inadvertently inflate performance by testing memorized data.
elements.sent-2411-029: We use multiple metrics to detect potential leakage.
elements.sent-2411-030: First, we investigate whether benchmark data has membership within \thestack, a widely-used used pretraining code dataset.
elements.sent-2411-031: Following this, we apply two core metrics for leakage from prior work~\cite{xu2024benchmarkingbenchmarkleakagelarge,li2023estimatingcontaminationperplexityquantifying : Negative Log-Likelihood (\nll) and n-gram accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-032: We apply these metrics to both well-known bug benchmarks, and a new dataset of high-quality code repositories from 2024, which we mined from \github.
elements.sent-2411-033: This new dataset is less likely to have appeared in models' training, which allows us to compare model performance between potentially familiar data versus likely novel code.
elements.sent-2411-034: Our findings suggest that older models, in particular \cgen, exhibit very high $n$-gram accuracy and low \nll on benchmark data, indicating a higher likelihood of memorization.
elements.sent-2411-035: Our evidence suggests that newer models trained on more extensive datasets, like \llama, show less memorization.
elements.sent-2411-036: Nonetheless, across all metrics, models, and families, \defj\ --- arguably the most widely-used bug benchmark --- consistently exhibits the highest rate of potential memorization.
elements.sent-2411-037: These results underscore the importance of carefully selecting benchmarks to ensure reliable evaluations.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{figures/methodology.pdf} \caption{Overview of our methodology for detecting leakage. We collected bug benchmarks and unseen repositories from 2024. We evaluated \nll and $N$-gram accuracy on base models, and analyzed membership of the benchmarks in \thestack.} \label{fig:methodology}
elements.sent-2411-038: We select widely used bug benchmarks across common programming languages, gathering ground-truth files containing reference solutions for each bug fix.
elements.sent-2411-039: To provide a likely not-leaked dataset for comparison, we also curated a set of recent open-source repositories from \github.
elements.sent-2411-040: To collect benchmarks of interest, we reviewed \url{program-repair.org and selected highly starred benchmarks across three programming languages: \bugscpp~\cite{bugscpp , \defj~\cite{defects4j , and \bugspy~\cite{bugsinpy .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: We next included two recent datasets to serve as reference points: \gitbug~\cite{gitbug-java , which was recently published to address the leakage issue, and \swebench~\cite{swebench , due to its rising popularity for code-related tasks~\cite{xia2024agentlessdemystifyingllmbasedsoftware,wang2024openhandsopenplatformai,aleithan2024swebenchenhancedcodingbenchmark .}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: We choose \swebench instead of \swebenchfull due to computational constraints.
elements.sent-2411-043: We collected the ground truth files for each patched bug.
elements.sent-2411-044: To reduce computational costs and prevent bias towards particular files, we removed files with more than 85\% overlap to produce a unique sample set.
elements.sent-2411-045: Duplicate files appear when multiple bugs in a dataset occur in the same file (e.g., \defj in project Lang, Bugs 1 and 3 affect the same file).
elements.sent-2411-046: We kept the oldest file for consistency.
elements.sent-2411-047: We collected only fixed files since these correspond to the solutions a model may have memorized.
elements.figure-2411-003: \caption{ Evaluation benchmark statistics, including newer and older benchmarks to assess leakage reduction over time. } \begin{tabular}{lrrrrr} Benchmark Year \# Bugs LOC (k) \# Stars Language Defects4J (v1.5) 2019 438 321 736 Java BugsInPy 2020 493 1253 80 Python BugsC++ 2021 209 4297 42 C++ GitBug-Java 2023 199 257 26 Java SWEBenchLite 2023 300 3148 1954 Python New Java Repos 2024 - 132 >100 Java New Python Repos 2024 - 65 >100 Python \end{tabular} \label{tab:benchmark-details}
elements.sent-2411-048: We also collected a new dataset of 3214 \github repositories written in \java and \py.
elements.sent-2411-049: We targeted repositories from 2024 to reduce the likelihood that current state-of-the-art LLMs have seen them.
elements.sent-2411-050: To narrow our selection to likely high-quality, indicative repositories, we applied a minimum threshold of 100 stars, a proxy for community interest and engagement.
elements.sent-2411-051: To ensure that we were mining novel datasets for LLMs, we applied the MinHash technique~\cite{broderMinHash with Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH)~\cite{gionisLSH to filter repositories potentially overlapping with existing models' training data.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Because of code duplication, some files in new 2024 repositories might be identical to those in older repositories.
elements.sent-2411-053: As a result, we collected all repositories created between 2022 and the end of 2023 with greater than 100 stars to exclude the repositories from 2024 that contained data from these older, highly popular repositories.
elements.sent-2411-054: Finally, to manage computational resources, we randomly sampled files from the collected data (k=250) for our evaluation.
elements.figure-2411-004: [] \caption{Models used for evaluation, including their training budget in trillions of tokens, number of layers, and cutoff year.} \label{tab:model_info} \begin{tabular}{lrr} Model Tokens (T) Cutoff Year Codegen Multi (6B)~\cite{nijkamp2022codegen} 0.5 2022 CodeLlama (7B)~\cite{rozière2024codellamaopenfoundation} 2.5 2023 LlaMa 3.1 (8B / 70B)~\cite{touvron2023llama} 15.0 2024 StarCoder 2 (7B)~\cite{lozhkov2024starcoder2stackv2} 3.5 2024 Gemma 2 (2B / 27B)~\cite{gemmateam2024gemma2} 2.0 / 13.0 2024 CodeGemma (7B)~\cite{codegemmateam2024codegemma} 6.5 2024 Mistral (7B)~\cite{jiang2023mistral7b} - - \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-055: We select a combination of models used for fault localization~\cite{yang2023largelanguagemodelstestfree , program repair~\cite{silva2024repairllamaefficientrepresentationsfinetuned , and vulnerability detection~\cite{zhou2024largelanguagemodelvulnerability .}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: These models are from families of well-known code-related models, including \cgen, the \llama family, the \gemma family, \starcoder, and \mistral.
elements.sent-2411-057: Note, we could not find detailed information about the training data for \mistral's models, apart from anecdotal evidence on public forums.
elements.sent-2411-058: We follow strategies from prior work~\cite{xu2024benchmarkingbenchmarkleakagelarge,li2023estimatingcontaminationperplexityquantifying to evaluate models for potential data leakage.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Membership operates at the repository level; Negative Log Likelihood and N-gram accuracy, at the file level (i.e., the compute model familiarity with a given file).
elements.sent-2411-060: In the bug datasets, these are the fixed (patched) files; in our novel dataset, they are randomly sampled files (Section~\ref{sec:data-collection ).}
elements.sent-2411-061: Membership: If a repository is included in a widely used pretraining dataset, many models have probably seen that repository's code.
elements.sent-2411-062: We do not have direct access to, nor knowledge of, the training datasets for all evaluation models.
elements.sent-2411-063: However, we have partial information about the use of pretraining datasets, such as for open-source models, and closed-source models are likely to use them as well.
elements.sent-2411-064: We therefore assess membership via whether a benchmark's repositories are present in \thestack~\cite{kocetkov2022stack3tbpermissively , a dataset of permissibly licensed source code in 358 programming languages intended for training and fine-tuning code-based models.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Given its size and popularity, several models report having trained on it, such as \starcoder 2~\cite{lozhkov2024starcoder2stackv2 ; other closed-source models are likely to also use it.}
elements.sent-2411-066: We used the Am I in the Stack tool\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/spaces/bigcode/in-the-stack on each benchmark repository, across the several versions of \thestack.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: If the model has seen a data point during training, we expect it to have a lower \nll on that point compared to unseen data.
elements.sent-2411-068: If the model has encountered a data point many times during training, \nll is expected to be particularly low (i.e., close to zero) compared to arbitrary code.
elements.sent-2411-069: To compute \nll, we use the reference implementation publicly available on HuggingFace.\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/en/perplexity Calculating the exact \nll for lengthy sequences is usually impractical because LLMs are trained on the limited context (moreover, we cannot fit an entire sequence in memory).}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Therefore, we split lengthy solution files into overlapping chunks, processed them individually, and combined them using a striding technique.
elements.sent-2411-071: We use strides of $512$ tokens when a sequence does not fit into the model's context window.
elements.sent-2411-072: N-gram accuracy: $N$-gram accuracy measures the extent to which a model's output exactly matches a reference sequence at the level of $n$-grams (i.e., contiguous sequences of $n$ tokens).
elements.sent-2411-073: High $n$-gram accuracy indicates that the model's output closely resembles the reference text, suggesting memorization.
elements.sent-2411-074: We follow prior work~\cite{xu2024benchmarkingbenchmarkleakagelarge and use $5$-grams ($5$-grams strike a balance between compute efficiency and metric accuracy).}
elements.sent-2411-075: Since most files cannot fit the context window, we use striding to cover the entire sequence.
elements.sent-2411-076: Following Xu et al.~\cite{xu2024benchmarkingbenchmarkleakagelarge , we compute $5$-grams from five uniformly distributed starting points per stride.}
elements.sent-2411-077: For each starting point, we provide the model with the preceding context, and check whether the predicted string matches ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-078: This section presents results assessing possible leakage of bug benchmarks in base models, using the metrics described in \Cref{sec:leakage : membership in \thestack (Section~\ref{sec:results-membership ), Negative Log Likelihood (Section~\ref{sec:results-nll ), and $N$-gram accuracy (Section~\ref{sec:nesults-ngram ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: We also perform a regression analysis of model characteristics, NLL, and $N$-gram accuracy (Section~\ref{sec:regression ) to better understand characteristics of models that influence data leakage.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Percentage of repositories in each benchmark leaked in \thestack versions 1.0, 2.0 and 2.1.} \begin{tabular}{lrrr} Benchmark v1.0 (\%) v2.0 (\%) v2.1 (\%) GitBug-Java 61.1 42.6 38.9 BugsInPy 94.1 64.7 64.7 BugsC++ 60.9 60.9 65.2 Defects4J 80.0 80.0 80.0 SWEBench-Lite 83.3 91.7 83.3 \end{tabular} \label{tab:stack-membership}
elements.sent-2411-080: The table excludes our new Java and Python data from 2024, as \thestack only includes data to 2023.
elements.sent-2411-081: Of all repositories, the new \gitbug benchmark has the lowest membership.
elements.sent-2411-082: While membership does not necessarily mean a model trained on \thestack has seen a specific fixed file (e.g., if a bug-fixing patch was applied after the dataset's cut-off date), the model may still be familiar with a project's source code.
elements.sent-2411-083: This familiarity could lead to higher-quality patches or results.
elements.sent-2411-084: This is not inherently problematic but is a critical factor to consider when assessing the model's potential for generalization.
elements.figure-2411-007:
egin{subfigure}{.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/pplx_ratios/Codegen_6B_Multi_nll_ratios.pdf} \label{MLEDdet} \end{subfigure}  egin{subfigure}{.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/pplx_ratios/LLama_31_70B_nll_ratios.pdf} \label{energydetPSK} \end{subfigure}  egin{subfigure}{.36\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/pplx_ratios/Gemma_2_27B_nll_ratios.pdf} \label{velcomp} \end{subfigure} \caption{ Heatmap illustrating the relative 
ll ratios across datasets for the \cgen, \llama, and \gemma. Each cell represents the ratio of the 
ll for the dataset in the column to that of the dataset in the row. For example, the 
ll for new \java repos is $5.63	imes$ higher than that for \defj. Darker colors correspond to higher ratios. } \label{fig:heatmap}
elements.sent-2411-085: Note that Negative Log Likelihood (\nll) depends on tokenization and architecture.
elements.sent-2411-086: This means we can only directly compare \nll values within model families.
elements.sent-2411-087: This strongly suggests potential data leakage.
elements.sent-2411-088: This is particularly evident with \cgen, which has very low \nll (0.15) for \defj.
elements.sent-2411-089: This matches our observations in \Cref{fig:motivation and suggests that \cgen has memorized the \defj solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-090: We observe comparably low \nll (0.38) on the \gemma 27B model for \defj relative to other benchmarks and repositories.
elements.sent-2411-091: This is because the projects in the benchmark existed prior to benchmark publication, as we also saw in the membership analysis (\Cref{tab:stack-membership ).}
elements.sent-2411-092: Moreover, although \swebench is a new benchmark, the bug fixes date as early as 2017.
elements.sent-2411-093: For all other models, the \nll values are fairly consistent across non-\defj benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-094: As expected, the new repositories we collected exhibit higher \nll compared to \defj, \bugscpp, \bugspy, and \swebench.
elements.sent-2411-095: Evaluation benchmarks are derived from prominent projects and may have been seen at a pretraining time multiple times, unlike our new repositories, which likely were not.
elements.sent-2411-096: Note, however, a potential confound, which is that our new repositories may be different in distribution compared to the models' training data, which may contribute to higher \nll.
elements.sent-2411-097: For example, \cgen's \nll on \defj is $5.63\times$ lower than on new \java repositories, and $3.82\times$ lower than on \gitbug.
elements.sent-2411-098: This ratio highlights a significant level of familiarity with \defj compared to new repositories.
elements.sent-2411-099: Newer models, particularly \llama, exhibit relatively consistent \nll values across all benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-100: For example, the \nll ratio between \defj and \gitbug for \llama 70B is only $1.27$, indicating that \llama perceives \defj patches as only slightly more predictable than \gitbug.
elements.sent-2411-101: This is expected, as the newer \llama family of models was trained on significantly more data and is thus less prone to data memorization.
elements.sent-2411-102: Specifically, the \llama family was trained on $30\times$ more tokens than \cgen.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{figures/grpupedbar_5gram_horiz.png} \caption{5-gram accuracy by model and dataset. For each model family (see \Cref{sec:model-selection}), we report 5-gram accuracy of either the only model available (e.g., \starcoder) or the biggest model in the family (e.g., \llama 70B).} \label{fig:ngram-results}
elements.sent-2411-103: Note that, due to differences in model vocabularies and tokenization, interpretation of $5$-gram match (similar to \nll) can differ across models.
elements.sent-2411-104: Conversely, $5$-gram accuracy on \gitbug is relatively similar to that of new repositories for all models, which aligns with the expectation that most repositories in the \gitbug benchmark were not included in pretraining data (as detailed in \Cref{tab:benchmark-details ).}
elements.sent-2411-105: For example, both \cgen and \gemma show significantly higher $5$-gram match differences between \defj and new repositories (i.e., $34$ \text{percentage points and $14$ \text{percentage points , respectively).}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Moreover, \cgen achieves over $82\%$ $5$-gram match on \defj, strongly suggesting that it has likely memorized much of the benchmark's solutions.
elements.sent-2411-107: As expected, new repositories generally exhibit lower $5$-gram match across all models, with averages of 47\% and 48\% for \java and \py, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-108: These values happen likely due to the presence of common coding patterns~\cite{gabel2010Uniqueness .}
elements.sent-2411-109: Conversely, and in line with our \nll findings, \cgen shows a notably high $5$-gram match on \swebench, even though it is a recently published dataset, as it incorporates older data.
elements.sent-2411-110: When it comes to \llama family, both \llama 70B and \starcoder exhibit consistent $5$-gram accuracy across benchmarks, which could suggest that these models are less prone to memorization due to their exposure to substantially larger datasets during pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-111: However, it is important to notice that $5$-gram accuracy alone may not conclusively prove an absence of memorization (contrary to high $n$-gram accuracy which reliably indicates strong pattern retention).
elements.sent-2411-112: For example, \Cref{fig:codegenexamplellama shows an example where \llama 70B is prompted to predict a patch from \bugspy (fastapi:Bug 12).}
elements.sent-2411-113: On this file, one of the expected $5$-grams is ``\_\_init\_\_(self," , and here \llama predicts ``\_\_init\_\_(\textbackslash nself," instead.
elements.sent-2411-114: This causes the $5$-grams not to match, even though the content is essentially the same.
elements.sent-2411-115: Therefore, it is crucial to evaluate models by considering \nll, $5$-gram accuracy, and membership as whole, as no single metric provides a complete picture of data leakage.
elements.subsection-2411-007: How Do Model Characteristics Influence Risk of Leakage?
elements.sent-2411-116: To gain deeper insights, and using the data collected during the study, we estimate regression models for negative log-likelihood (\nll) and 5-gram accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-117: The regressions allow us to explore relationships and (potentially) causality that metrics alone cannot reveal.
elements.sent-2411-118: For example, while \cgen presents low \nll values and high 5-gram accuracy, it is unclear what drives this outcome.
elements.sent-2411-119: By employing regression analysis, we can identify factors such as training parameters and budget as significant influences on these metrics.
elements.sent-2411-120: Therefore, for \nll, we use a mixed-effects linear model, while for 5-gram accuracy (i.e., a probability), we use a mixed-effects logistic regression.
elements.sent-2411-121: As predictors, we include the model's number of parameters and number of tokens used during pretraining (i.e., training budget), which serves as a proxy for the unique token count.
elements.sent-2411-122: We acknowledge that reporting the exact number of unique tokens would be a more precise metric, but such data is often unavailable.
elements.sent-2411-123: We also account for potential variability caused by differences in the datasets by including dataset type as random effect.. \Cref{tab:regression shows results.}
elements.sent-2411-124: For \nll, the intercept represents the average \nll value expected when all predictions are zero (1.497).
elements.sent-2411-125: The results suggest that increasing the training budget by one trillion tokens raises \nll by 0.091 units on solution files, while the effects of model parameters are negligible.
elements.sent-2411-126: For 5-gram accuracy, the intercept represents the log-odds when all predictors are zero, translating to a probability of approximately 63\% (i.e., $e^{-0.457}$).
elements.sent-2411-127: This suggests that, by default, the models achieve 63\% accuracy in predicting 5-grams.
elements.sent-2411-128: While this may initially seem like a high value, it's actually not surprising given that code is highly repetitive and follows natural patterns.
elements.sent-2411-129: The positive coefficient for parameters (0.003) indicates that increasing the number of parameters leads to higher 5-gram accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-130: Conversely, the training budget shows a highly significant negative effect (-0.035), suggesting that the likelihood of achieving higher 5-gram accuracy decreases (and, consequently, memorization) as the training budget increases.
elements.sent-2411-131: Concretely, \cgen has the smallest known training budget of all models evaluated, with approximately 0.5T tokens.
elements.sent-2411-132: Due to its smaller budget, the resulting model generalizes less across all seen code and is more susceptible to memorization, as seen in \Cref{fig:motivation .}
elements.sent-2411-133: The high $5$-gram match and low \nll support this intuition, especially for \defj.
elements.sent-2411-134: Conversely, newer models, like \starcoder, exhibit much lower memorization of \defj, despite its existence in all versions of \thestack.
elements.sent-2411-135: As \starcoder's training budget is significantly larger than \cgen's (3.5T tokens), \defj constitutes a smaller percentage of the overall training data.
elements.sent-2411-136: Regression results also imply that models with more parameters tend to exhibit higher n-gram accuracy and, consequently, memorize more.
elements.sent-2411-137: For example, \Cref{fig:codegenexamplellama shows that \llama 70B accurately predicts strings, class names, and if-statements in contexts where these predictions might not be immediately apparent, which may indicate memorization.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Conversely, while the 8B (\Cref{fig:codegenexamplellama2 ) version predicts the patch more accurately, it fails to identify the subsequent classes.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{Summary of regressions tests for negative log likelihood (\nll) and 5-gram accuracy. We report the coefficient estimates with their standard errors in parentheses. } \begin{tabular}{lrr} \multicolumn{1}{c}{\nll} \multicolumn{1}{c}{5-gram} Intercept 1.497 (0.251) *** -0.457 (0.020) *** Parameters $-6.2e^{-4}$ (0.003) \phantom{***} 0.003 ($7.0e^{-4}$) *** Training budget 0.091 (0.013) *** -0.035 (0.003) *** \multicolumn{3}{l}{Note: *** p-value \textless~0.001} \end{tabular} \label{tab:regression}
elements.sent-2411-139: Our evaluation provides compelling evidence that data leakage is an especially significant issue for \defj (V1.5).
elements.sent-2411-140: This is evident from the lower \nll values, higher 5-gram accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-141: In particular, \cgen achieves $82\%$ 5-gram accuracy on \defj, while both CodeLlama 7B and Gemma-2 27B attain $64\%$.
elements.sent-2411-142: Moreover, given that \defj is incorporated into the widely-used pretraining dataset (\thestack), with $80\%$ membership, eliminating this leakage in future models is likely nearly impossible.
elements.sent-2411-143: We also observe similarly low \nll values in \swebench for \cgen.
elements.sent-2411-144: Newer benchmarks \bugspy and \bugscpp exhibit lower leakage risk.
elements.sent-2411-145: A smaller percentage of their repositories are indexed in the latest version of \thestack.
elements.sent-2411-146: While \bugspy and \bugscpp exhibit slightly lower \nll compared to new repositories, their 5-gram accuracy and \nll values are not significantly different from newer, more-likely-unseen benchmarks like \gitbug.
elements.sent-2411-147: This suggests that, for now, researchers can use these benchmarks with a relatively low risk of data leakage.
elements.sent-2411-148: However, as pretraining datasets continue to evolve, we recommend that researchers regularly assess \thestack membership, $5$-gram match and \nll values of these benchmarks, especially compared to more recent data, to monitor and mitigate potential data contamination.
elements.sent-2411-149: We also observe that newer models, e.g., the \llama family, seem to have lower leakage (likely due to being trained on more data).
elements.sent-2411-150: Nonetheless, we still observed cases where \llama can output solution files despite little context (e.g., \Cref{fig:codegenexamplellama2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-151: We suggest researchers consider supplementing their evaluations with more recent benchmarks such as \gitbug.
elements.sent-2411-152: Benchmarks like \gitbug, which focus on recent bugs and patches, are less likely to have been included in pretraining datasets compared to established benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-153: Leveraging these newer benchmarks can provide more reliable evaluations for assessing model's capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-154: Data Collection: Despite efforts to filter out older \github repositories when collecting new data, we anecdotally observed instances where files appeared to be adaptations of existing files (e.g., from 2018\footnote{\url{https://bit.ly/3Od3nC6 .}}
elements.sent-2411-155: We moreover cannot guarantee that the new repositories are identically distributed compared to those in the benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-156: To mitigate this issue, given that the repositories in the benchmarks tend to be highly recognizable projects, we applied a >100-star filter to argue that the selected projects are of comparable quality.
elements.sent-2411-157: Train + Test Splits: For benchmarks like \defj (v1.5) and \bugspy, the patch files and buggy files are very similar (typically a patch involves only changing a small number of lines).
elements.sent-2411-158: LLMs may have only seen the train split of these benchmarks at pretraining time.
elements.sent-2411-159: This would result in high 5-gram match and low \nll on patch files, even if only buggy files were leaked.
elements.sent-2411-160: Nonetheless, we observed multiple cases where models output patched files verbatim.
elements.sent-2411-161: We mitigate this by looking at trends in NLL and 5-gram match rather than absolute numbers in our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-162: Forgetting: The findings presented in this paper primarily address leakage in the context of base models.
elements.sent-2411-163: Empirical results show that models can ``forget" portions of their pretraining data during fine-tuning~\cite{luo2024empiricalstudycatastrophicforgetting .}
elements.sent-2411-164: That said, while full-scale model fine-tuning may reduce leakage risks, more recent fine-tuning strategies—such as the addition of adapter layers—often "freeze" pretrained weights.
elements.sent-2411-165: This practice can inadvertently increase the likelihood of data leakage.
elements.sent-2411-166: Furthermore, larger models exhibit a stronger tendency to memorize training data~\cite{tirumala2022memorizationoverfittinganalyzingtraining , suggesting that while fine-tuning may mitigate leakage for smaller models, it is more likely to exacerbate leakage concerns in larger models.}
elements.sent-2411-167: We leave empirical assessment of these risks to future work.
elements.sent-2411-168: Large Language Models (LLM): LLMs have shown promise across a wide range of natural language~\cite{rajpurkar2016squad100000questionsmachine,hendrycks2021measuringmassivemultitasklanguage and code generation tasks~\cite{chen2021evaluatinglargelanguagemodels, austin2021programsynthesislargelanguage, hendrycks2021measuringcodingchallengecompetence .}}
elements.sent-2411-169: Current state of the art LLMs include open source models such as CodeGen, CodeLlama, LlaMa 3.1, Gemma and PhiCoder and closed-source models such as GPT-4o, Claude 3.5, and Gemini 1.5.
elements.sent-2411-170: LLMs have shown state of the art performance in APR, suggesting patches to buggy code segments, detecting vulnerabilities and helping pinpoint buggy lines in a piece of code~\cite{yang2023largelanguagemodelstestfree, silva2024repairllamaefficientrepresentationsfinetuned, xia2023conversationgoingfixing162 .}
elements.sent-2411-171: Despite the widespread success of LLMs in APR tasks, there are still significant concerns regarding data leakage.
elements.sent-2411-172: This is prevalent for LLMs trained on large, publicly available code repositories, as these models might "remember" solutions from benchmark datasets, resulting in inflated performance.
elements.sent-2411-173: Measuring Data Leakage: This task is still an open challenge.
elements.sent-2411-174: Three common ways to measure data leakage are perplexity or \nll, n-gram match, and model performance.
elements.sent-2411-175: Perplexity approaches ~\cite{li2023estimatingcontaminationperplexityquantifying, xu2024benchmarkingbenchmarkleakagelarge quantify leakage by comparing loss across benchmarks.}
elements.sent-2411-176: N-gram approaches~\cite{xu2024benchmarkingbenchmarkleakagelarge,shi2024detectingpretrainingdatalarge , prompt a model with context and measure the overlap of generated code with the ground truth.}
elements.sent-2411-177: Finally, performance approaches~\cite{jain2024livecodebenchholisticcontaminationfree, zuoLeakage measure the difference in performance between a benchmark and a transformed version of the benchmark.}
elements.sent-2411-178: While we use techniques from the literature, we are the first to apply these metrics to bug benchmarks widely used in the software engineering community.
elements.sent-2411-179: We avoid measuring performance with transformations, as base models perform very poorly on untransformed bug benchmarks without fine-tuning (e.g.: we experimented with \llama 70B and achieved nearly 0\% performance at fault localization on \defj).
elements.sent-2411-180: In this paper, we measure data leakage risks across multiple widely used bug benchmarks and state-of-the-art open and closed-source models.
elements.sent-2411-181: Older benchmarks, especially \defj, exhibit higher memorization signals in models, particularly in smaller, older models with lower training budgets like \cgen 6B.
elements.sent-2411-182: Newer datasets such as \gitbug, \bugspy, \bugscpp show lower leakage risk, with similar \nll and 5-gram match to new 2024 repositories.
elements.sent-2411-183: We also find that newer models with higher training budgets display significantly lower risks of data leakage.
elements.sent-2411-184: Our findings suggest that researchers should consider both the models they use in evaluation and pair older benchmarks, such as \defj, with more recent benchmarks, such as \gitbug, to minimize data contamination risk.. \bibliographystyle{IEEETran \appendices }

elements.paragraph-2411-003: NLL ratios across models and benchmarks
elements.figure-2411-015: \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/appendix/CodeGemma_7B_nll_ratios.pdf} \caption{NLL ratios for CodeGemma 7B.} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/appendix/Mistral_7B_nll_ratios.pdf} \caption{NLL ratios for Mistral 7B.} \end{subfigure}
elements.figure-2411-016: \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/appendix/Llama_8B_nll_ratios.pdf} \caption{NLL ratios for LlaMa 3.1 8B.} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/appendix/Gemma_2B_nll_ratios.pdf} \caption{NLL ratios for Gemma 2B.} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/appendix/CodeLLama_7B_nll_ratios.pdf} \caption{NLL ratios for CodeLlaMa 7B.} \end{subfigure} \label{fig:appendix-subfigures}
elements.sent-2411-185: To identify potential leakage, we use multiple metrics, including a study of benchmark membership within commonly used training datasets, as well as analyses of negative log-likelihood and \fivegram.
elements.sent-2411-186: Following this, we apply two core metrics for leakage from prior work~\cite{xu2024benchmarkingbenchmarkleakagelarge,li2023estimatingcontaminationperplexityquantifying : Negative Log-Likelihood (\nll) and \fivegram.}
elements.sent-2411-187: Our findings suggest that older models, in particular \cgen, exhibit very high \fivegram and low \nll on benchmark data, indicating a higher likelihood of memorization.
elements.sent-2411-188: Nonetheless, across all metrics, and models, \defj\ --- arguably the most widely-used bug benchmark --- consistently exhibits the highest rate of potential memorization.
elements.sent-2411-189: To provide a likely not-leaked datasets for comparison, we also curated a set of recent open-source repositories from \github.
elements.sent-2411-190: Finally, to manage computational resources, we randomly sampled files from the collected repositories ($250$ files per language) for our evaluation.
elements.figure-2411-017: [] \caption{Models used for evaluation, including their training budget in trillions of tokens, number of layers, and cutoff year.} \label{tab:model_info} \begin{tabular}{lrr} Model Tokens (T) Cutoff Year Codegen Multi (6B)~\cite{nijkamp2022codegen} 1 2022 CodeLlama (7B)~\cite{rozière2024codellamaopenfoundation} 2.5 2023 LlaMa 3.1 (8B / 70B)~\cite{touvron2023llama} 15.0 2024 StarCoder 2 (7B)~\cite{lozhkov2024starcoder2stackv2} 3.5 2024 Gemma 2 (2B / 27B)~\cite{gemmateam2024gemma2} 2.0 / 13.0 2024 CodeGemma (7B)~\cite{codegemmateam2024codegemma} 6.5 2024 Mistral (7B)~\cite{jiang2023mistral7b} - - \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-191: Note that we focus on open-source base models because our method requires computing the negative log-likelihood (\nll) on sequences, which is generally not possible with closed-source models.
elements.sent-2411-192: We exclude instruction-tuned models and concentrate solely on pretrained models before fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-193: Since instruction-tuned models are optimized for conversational formats, \ngram may be a less suitable metric for measuring memorization in these models.
elements.sent-2411-194: This section presents results assessing possible leakage of bug benchmarks in base models, using the metrics described in \Cref{sec:leakage : membership in \thestack (Section~\ref{sec:results-membership ), Negative Log Likelihood (Section~\ref{sec:results-nll ), and \fivegram (Section~\ref{sec:nesults-ngram ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: We also perform a regression analysis of model characteristics, NLL, and \fivegram (Section~\ref{sec:regression ) to better understand characteristics of models that influence data leakage.}
elements.figure-2411-018:
egin{subfigure}{.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/appendix/Codegen6BMultinllNOBAR.pdf} \label{MLEDdet} \end{subfigure}  egin{subfigure}{.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/appendix/LLama70BnllNOBAR.pdf} \label{energydetPSK} \end{subfigure}  egin{subfigure}{.36\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/appendix/Gemma27Bnll.pdf} \label{velcomp} \end{subfigure} \caption{ Heatmap illustrating the relative 
ll ratios across datasets for the \cgen, \llama, and \gemma. Each cell represents the ratio of the 
ll for the dataset in the column to that of the dataset in the row. For example, the 
ll for new \java repos is $5.63	imes$ higher than that for \defj. Darker colors correspond to higher ratios. } \label{fig:heatmap}
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{figures/grpupedbar_5gram_horiz.png} \caption{\fivegram by model and dataset. Due to space constraints, we selected a sample of the most relevant models. \fivegram for other models are consistent with the results displayed. } \label{fig:ngram-results}
elements.sent-2411-196: Note that, due to differences in model vocabularies and tokenization, interpretation of \fivegram can differ across models.
elements.sent-2411-197: Conversely, \fivegram on \gitbug is relatively similar to that of new repositories for all models, which aligns with the expectation that most repositories in the \gitbug benchmark were not included in pretraining data (as detailed in \Cref{tab:benchmark-details ).}
elements.sent-2411-198: Moreover, \cgen achieves $82\%$ \fivegram on \defj, strongly suggesting that it has likely memorized much of the benchmark's solutions.
elements.sent-2411-199: As expected, new repositories generally exhibit lower \fivegram across all models, with averages of 47\% and 48\% for \java and \py, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-200: Conversely, and in line with our \nll findings, \cgen shows a notably high \fivegram on \swebench, even though it is a recently published dataset, as it incorporates~older data.
elements.sent-2411-201: When it comes to \llama family, both \llama 70B and 8B exhibit consistent \fivegram across benchmarks, which could suggest that these models are less prone to memorization due to their exposure to substantially larger datasets during pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-202: However, it is important to notice that \fivegram alone may not conclusively prove an absence of memorization (contrary to high $n$-gram accuracy which reliably indicates strong pattern retention).
elements.sent-2411-203: Therefore, it is crucial to evaluate models by considering \nll, \fivegram, and membership as whole, as no single metric provides a complete picture of data leakage.
elements.sent-2411-204: To gain deeper insights, and using the data collected during the study, we estimate regression models for the average values of \nll and \fivegram.
elements.sent-2411-205: The regressions allow us to explore relationships that metrics alone cannot reveal.
elements.sent-2411-206: Using regression analysis, we can identify factors such as training parameters and budget as significant influences on these metrics.
elements.sent-2411-207: Therefore, we use a simple mixed-effects linear model to predict the average \nll and \fivegram.
elements.sent-2411-208: As predictors, we include the models' number of parameters and number of tokens used during pretraining (i.e., training budget), which serves as a proxy for the unique token count.
elements.sent-2411-209: We also account for potential variability caused by differences in the datasets and tokenizers by including dataset and tokenizer type as random effect.. \Cref{tab:regression shows results.}
elements.sent-2411-210: In a linear regression, the intercept represents the predicted value of the dependent variable (averages of \nll and \fivegram) when all predictors are at their reference values.
elements.sent-2411-211: To make the interpretation easier, we centered the predictors around \cgen values.
elements.sent-2411-212: Therefore, the reference value of parameters is 6B and the reference value of training budget is 1T tokens.
elements.sent-2411-213: The regression coefficients represent the change in the dependent variable for a one-unit increase in the predictor, holding other predictors constant.
elements.sent-2411-214: For \nll, the predicted average is 0.744 when at reference level, i.e., parameters = 6B and training budget = 1T.
elements.sent-2411-215: For every 1B increase in number of parameters (above 6B), the predicted \nll average decreases by $0.002$ units (while holding training budget constant, at 1T).
elements.sent-2411-216: Similarly, for every 1T tokens increase in training budget, predicted \nll decreases by $0.014$ units.
elements.sent-2411-217: For \fivegram, the predicted average is 0.465 when predictors are at reference level.
elements.sent-2411-218: For every 1B increase in number of parameters, the predicted \fivegram increases by $0.001$ units.
elements.sent-2411-219: Similarly, a 1-unit increase in the training budget leads to an increase of $0.006$ in \fivegram.
elements.sent-2411-220: All reported coefficients have statistical significance.
elements.sent-2411-221: Regression results reveal consistent trends across model families (e.g., \llama or Gemma).
elements.sent-2411-222: For example, both \llama 8B and 70B were trained on the same data with a training budget of 15T tokens.
elements.sent-2411-223: However, the 70B model is approximately ten times larger than the 8B model, leading to an overall increase in \fivegram across all benchmarks (as shown in \Cref{fig:ngram-results ) and decrease in \nll.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Similarly, within the \gemma family, the 2B model was trained on 3T tokens, while the 27B was trained on 12T.
elements.sent-2411-225: Here, we observe the same trend: average \fivegram increases in the 27B model and \nll decreases.
elements.sent-2411-226: Regression results also imply that models with more parameters tend to exhibit higher \ngram and, consequently, memorize more.
elements.sent-2411-227: For example, \Cref{fig:codegenexamplellama shows that \llama 70B, which has the same training budget as \llama 8B, accurately predicts strings, class names, and if-statements in contexts where these predictions might not be immediately apparent, which may indicate memorization.}
elements.figure-2411-020: \caption{Summary of regressions tests for negative log likelihood (\nll) and \fivegram. We report the coefficient estimates with their standard errors in parentheses. } \begin{tabular}{lrr} \multicolumn{1}{c}{\nll} \multicolumn{1}{c}{5-gram} Intercept 0.744 (0.094) *** 0.465 (0.049) *** Parameters -0.002 (0.001) *\phantom{**} $0.001$ ($4.0 e{-4}$) *\phantom{**} Training budget -0.014 (0.004) **\phantom{*} $0.006$ ($2.4 e{-3}$) *\phantom{**} \multicolumn{3}{l}{Note: *** p-value \textless~0.001, ** p-value \textless~0.01, * p-value \textless~0.05} \end{tabular} \label{tab:regression}
elements.sent-2411-228: This is evident from the lower \nll values and higher \fivegram.
elements.sent-2411-229: In particular, \cgen achieves $82\%$ \fivegram on \defj, while both CodeLlama 7B and Gemma-2 27B attain $64\%$.
elements.sent-2411-230: Newer benchmarks \bugspy and \bugscpp exhibit lower leakage risk in almost all models.
elements.sent-2411-231: While \bugspy and \bugscpp exhibit slightly lower \nll compared to new repositories, their \fivegram and \nll values are not significantly different from newer, more-likely-unseen benchmarks like \gitbug.
elements.sent-2411-232: However, as pretraining datasets continue to evolve, we recommend that researchers regularly assess \thestack membership, \fivegram and \nll values of these benchmarks, especially compared to more recent data, to monitor and mitigate potential data contamination.
elements.sent-2411-233: We also observe that the \llama family seems to exhibit lower memorization of benchmark solutions.
elements.sent-2411-234: Nonetheless, we still observed cases where \llama 70B outputs solution files despite little context (e.g., \Cref{fig:example_ngrams_semantic_memorization ).}
elements.sent-2411-235: Train + Test Splits: For benchmarks like \defj (V1.5) and \bugspy, the patch files and buggy files are very similar (typically a patch involves only changing a small number of lines).
elements.sent-2411-236: This would result in high \fivegram and low \nll on patch files, even if only buggy files were leaked.
elements.sent-2411-237: We mitigate this by looking at trends in NLL and \fivegram rather than absolute numbers in our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-238: Empirical results show that models can ``forget'' portions of their pretraining data during fine-tuning~\cite{luo2024empiricalstudycatastrophicforgetting .}
elements.sent-2411-239: That said, while full-scale model fine-tuning may reduce leakage risks, more recent fine-tuning strategies---such as the addition of adapter layers---often ``freeze'' pretrained weights.
elements.sent-2411-240: Current state of the art LLMs include open source models such as \cgen, \codellama, \llama, \gemma and and closed-source models such as GPT-4o, Claude 3.5, and Gemini 1.5.
elements.sent-2411-241: Three common ways to measure data leakage are perplexity or \nll, \ngram, and model performance.
elements.sent-2411-242: While we build on established techniques from the literature, our work is the first to apply these metrics to bug benchmarks widely recognized in the software engineering community.
elements.sent-2411-243: Additionally, we conduct a comprehensive study of both recent models, such as the \llama family, and earlier models like \cgen.
elements.sent-2411-244: In this paper, we measure data leakage risks across multiple widely used bug benchmarks and state-of-the-art open-source models.
elements.sent-2411-245: Older benchmarks, especially \defj, exhibit higher memorization signals across all models, particularly in \cgen 6B.
elements.sent-2411-246: Newer datasets such as \gitbug, \bugspy, \bugscpp show lower leakage risk, with similar \nll and \fivegram to new 2024 repositories.
elements.sent-2411-247: Our findings suggest that researchers should consider both the models they use in evaluation and pair older benchmarks, such as \defj, with more recent benchmarks, such as \gitbug, to improve model evaluation and address potential leakage issues appropriately.

elements.sent-2411-248: We collected a new dataset of 3,214 \github repositories written in \java (1,183) and \py (2,031), targeting repositories from 2024 to reduce the likelihood that state-of-the-art LLMs have seen them.
elements.sent-2411-249: To focus on high-quality repositories, we applied a 100-star minimum threshold as a proxy for community interest.
elements.sent-2411-250: To ensure novelty, we used MinHash~\cite{broderMinHash with Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH)~\cite{gionisLSH to filter repositories overlapping with existing training data.}}
elements.sent-2411-251: Due to code duplication, some files in 2024 repositories might overlap with older repositories.
elements.sent-2411-252: Therefore, we included repositories from 2022–2023 (>100 stars) to exclude 2024 repositories containing duplicated data.
elements.sent-2411-253: Finally, we randomly sampled 250 files per language to manage computational resources for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-254: These models are from families of well-known code-related models, including \cgen, \llama, \gemma, \starcoder, and \mistral.
elements.sent-2411-255: This causes the $5$-grams not to match, even though the content is the same.
elements.sent-2411-256: Regression results reveal consistent trends across model families.
elements.sent-2411-257: Data Collection: Despite efforts to filter out older \github repositories when collecting new data, we anecdotally observed instances where files appeared to be adaptations of existing files (e.g., from 2018) Our filtering process may not have perfectly excluded legacy code.
elements.sent-2411-258: Our findings suggest that researchers should consider both the models they use in evaluation and pair older benchmarks, such as \defj, with more recent benchmarks, such as \gitbug, to improve model evaluation and address potential leakage issues.
elements.sent-2411-259: The authors would like to thank Luke Dramko for providing code that we used for deduplicating 2024 repositories.
